coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots for cancer targeted drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Iannazzo, Daniela
Pistone, Alessandro
Salamò, Marina
Galvagno, Signorino
Romeo, Roberto
Giofré, Salvatore V.
Branca, Caterina
Visalli, Giuseppa
Di Pietro, Angela
coredata.dc:description: A biocompatible and cell traceable drug delivery system Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) based, for the targeted delivery of the DNA intercalating drug doxorubicin (DOX) to cancer cells, is here reported. Highly dispersible and water soluble GQD, synthesized by acidic oxidation and exfoliation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), were covalently linked to the tumor targeting module biotin (BTN), able to efficiently recognize biotin receptors over-expressed on cancer cells and loaded with DOX. Biological test performed on A549 cells reported a very low toxicity of the synthesized carrier (GQD and GQD-BTN). In GQD-BTN-DOX treated cancer cells, the cytotoxicity was strongly dependent from cell uptake which was greater and delayed after treatment with GQD-BTN-DOX system with respect to what observed for cells treated with the same system lacking of the targeting module BTN (GQD-DOX) or with the free drug alone. A delayed nuclear internalization of the drug is reported, due to the drug detachment from the nanosystem, triggered by the acidic environment of cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery system
Graphene quantum dots
Targeted delivery
Anticancer activity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GQD for Tumor Targeted Doxorubicin Delivery.

(A) XRD patterns of GQD and p-MWCNT; (B) Representative HRTEM images of GQD; (C) Particle size distribution as extracted by CONTIN analysis of DLS data of GQD sample; (D) PL spectra of GQD dispersion in deionized water at the excitation wavelengths of 310, 320, 330, 340, 350, 360 and 370.
Synthesis of GQD-BTN-DOX. Reagents and conditions: (a) BTN, EDC·HCl, HOBt, DMAP, CH2Cl2, 4d, r.t.; (b) Doxorubicin, buffer solution pH 7.4, 24h, r.t.

Characterization of GQD (black line), GQD-BTN (blue line) and GQD-BTN-DOX (red line). (A) TGA profiles of GQD, GQD-BTN and GQD-BTN-DOX; (B) IR spectra of GQD, GQD-BTN and GQD-BTN-DOX. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PL spectra of GQD (black line), GQD-BTN (blue line), GQD-BTN-DOX (red line) and DOX (pink line) in deionized water, after excitation at 360nm. The concentration of all the investigated samples was 100ng/mL. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Biocompatibility of GQD and GQD-BTN, assessed after treatment for 24h, measuring cell viability, expressed as the percentage of viable cells relative to the untreated control cells (arbitrarily set at 100). The percentage of viable cells is plotted versus the concentration (10–100μg/mL). Viability was evaluated by cytoflurimetric analysis, measuring emission signals in the FL-3 channel of cell suspensions (∼5×104 mL−1) loaded by propidium iodine (3μg/mL); (B) Doxorubicin uptake in A549 cancer cell line after treatment for 4h with GQD-DOX, GQD-BTN-DOX and free DOX at the same doses present in the loaded nanocarriers. The average emission values of GQD-BTN-DOX were higher that when the cells are treated with free drug or GQD-DOX. In GQD-BTN-DOX (50μg/mL) treated cells the value was 27% higher.

CLSM images to evaluate the druginternalization for free DOX (4h) and GQD-BTN-DOX treated cells, at 4h and 6h. In the right panel the red fluorescence of DNA intercalating drug allows to evaluate its localization in cell compartments. In the left panel the correspondent brightfield images. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots for cancer targeted drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, University of Messina, Contrada Di Dio, I-98166 Messina, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical, Biological, Pharmaceutical and Environmental Sciences, University of Messina, Viale Annunziata, I-98168 Messina, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical, Biological, Pharmaceutical and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mathematical and Computer Sciences, Physical Sciences and Earth Sciences, Viale F. Stagno d’Alcontres, 98166 Messina, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mathematical and Computer Sciences, Physical Sciences and Earth Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical and Dental Sciences and Morphological and Functional Images, University Hospital of Messina, Via Consolare Valeria, 1, 98100, Messina, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomedical and Dental Sciences and Morphological and Functional Images

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A biocompatible and cell traceable drug delivery system Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) based, for the targeted delivery of the DNA intercalating drug doxorubicin (DOX) to cancer cells, is here reported. Highly dispersible and water soluble GQD, synthesized by acidic oxidation and exfoliation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), were covalently linked to the tumor targeting module biotin (BTN), able to efficiently recognize biotin receptors over-expressed on cancer cells and loaded with DOX. Biological test performed on A549 cells reported a very low toxicity of the synthesized carrier (GQD and GQD-BTN). In GQD-BTN-DOX treated cancer cells, the cytotoxicity was strongly dependent from cell uptake which was greater and delayed after treatment with GQD-BTN-DOX system with respect to what observed for cells treated with the same system lacking of the targeting module BTN (GQD-DOX) or with the free drug alone. A delayed nuclear internalization of the drug is reported, due to the drug detachment from the nanosystem, triggered by the acidic environment of cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticle based Drug Delivery Systems (DDS) have shown unprecedented opportunity in cancer treatments for improving drug loading, targeting and efficacy (Liu et al., 2016). Innovative nanotechnology platforms, such as polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, dendrimers, nanoshells, carbon nanotubes, superparamagnetic and nucleic acid based nanoparticles, have been employed to the targeted delivery of biologically active cargoes into living systems, showing improved safety profile and enhanced antitumor efficacy when compared with the free drugs (Nazir et al., 2014; Radenkovica et al., 2016). Among the different classes of nanomaterials the carbon allotropes, fullerene, carbon nanotubes and graphene have been widely used in biomedical field as biosensors, imaging probes, and DDS (Marchesan and Prato, 2013; Iannazzo et al., 2013, 2015a). Recently, graphene and graphene oxide (GO) aroused significant interest in biomedical research because of their single atomic-layered structure and chemical properties (Zhang et al., 2012; Iannazzo et al., 2015b). Functionalized graphene and GO have shown to improve the drugs solubility, to extend their half-life, and to reduce their side effects (Zhao et al., 2014).

Graphene quantum dots (GQD), fragments limited in size of a single-layer two-dimensional graphene, are considered as the next generation of carbon-based nanomaterials with enormous potential in biomedical field (Shen et al., 2012). Recent studies on this class of nanomaterials report that GQD are less toxic and more hydrophobic with respect to graphene and endowed of stable strong fluorescence (Zhu et al., 2013). Their intrinsic fluorescence have attracted increasing attention in anticancer therapy since it may allow the efficient tracking of human cells in vitro (Chen et al., 2013). Analogously to graphene and GO, the presence of more active groups on the GQD surface allows their multimodal conjugation, making them ideal carriers for the simultaneous treatment and tracking of cancer cells. Moreover, due to their unique structural properties, GQD have shown to increase chemotherapy efficacy of anticancer drugs that are suboptimal due to the drug resistance (Wang et al., 2013). Recent works demonstrated that GQD can efficiently accelerate the nuclear accumulation of drugs, such as doxorubicin or cisplatin and also enhance markedly the DNA cleavage activity and cytotoxicity of these drugs. These outstanding biological properties, highlight the superiority of GQD over the modified graphene or GO and many other nanoparticle based delivery systems (Sui et al., 2016).

However, the design of effective DDS to treat cancer should include a tumor targeting ligand unit that could specifically recognize cancer receptors on the cell surface and induce receptor-mediated endocytosis, in order to minimize systemic toxicity and undesirable side effects, typically associated with conventional chemotherapy. Targeting ligands such as arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (RGD), folic acid, biotin and antibodies have been inserted to carbon based DDS and have shown to improve therapeutic responses both in vitro and in vivo (Chen et al., 2008).

Based on these considerations, the aim of this study was to design a new biocompatible and cell traceable DDS, able to release the therapeutic agent to cancer cells in a selective manner. To achieve our goal we have used as nanocarrier, highly dispersible and water soluble GQD, synthesized by acidic oxidation and exfoliation of purified pristine multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) where the presence of numerous defects in graphene layers can greatly contribute to afford GQD with a great oxidation extent and water solubility (Lavin et al., 2002).

GQD were covalently linked to the tumor targeting module biotin (vitamin K or vitamin B7), able to efficiently recognize biotin receptors over-expressed on cancer cellsby means of a strategically designed cleavable linker which can be specifically activated inside cells (Russel-Jones et al., 2004). The therapeutic agent used in this study, doxorubicin (DOX), was loaded to the GQD surface, taking advantage of the excellent absorption properties of carbon nanomaterials, by π-π interaction; moreover its inherent fluorescence allowed the drug release to be tracked (Fig. 1 ).

Solvents and reagents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. MWCNTs were produced by catalytic chemical vapor deposition (CCVD) from isobutane on a Fe/Al2O3 catalyst; synthesized MWCNTs were then subjected to purification, as reported previously, giving pristine MWCNT (p-MWCNTs) with purity>95%.

Pristine MWCNT were treated with a HNO3/H2SO4 mixture in a 1:3 ratio (Donato et al., 2009); the mixture was placed in a reaction flask equipped with a condenser and the suspension was refluxed and sonicated in an ultrasonic water bath at 60°C, for 4days. The mixture was then diluted with deionized water and filtered under vacuum on 0,1μm Millipore membrane. The filtrate was neutralized with NaOH and centrifuged at 3000rpm. The resulting material was washed with deionized water until no salts were present in the washing solutions and dried at 60°C under vacuum. The amount of carboxylic groups present on the nanomaterial was evaluated by Boehm titration, using NaHCO3 as titrating agent (Oickle et al., 2010).

To a dispersion of biotin (0,409mmol) in tetrahydrofuran, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl 0.491mmol) and triethylammine (0.491mmol) were added and the mixture was left under stirring for 1h. 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBt, 66mg, 0.491mmol) was added and the mixture was stirred for 1h more; then, tert-butyl-2-(2-(2-aminoethoxy) ethoxy) ethylcarbamate, synthesized according to a previously reported procedure (Iannazzo et al., 2012), was added and the suspension was left under stirring for 24h at room temperature. After removal of the solvent, CH2Cl2 was added and the organic phase was washed with water, dried over sodium sulfate, filtered, and evaporated to dryness. The residue was purified by MPLC on a silica gel column using as eluent a mixture of CH2Cl2/MeOH (9:1) to afford the BTN module protected at the amino functionality, in 95% yield. The N-BOC protected BTN module (70mg, 0.147mmol) was dissolved in CH2Cl2 and added with trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, 0.295mmol); the solution was left under stirring at room temperature for 1h. Then, toluene was added to form a TFA azeotrope and the solvent was removed under vacuum. The residue was purified by MPLC on a silica gel column using as eluent a mixture of CH2Cl2/MeOH (9:1) to afford BTN in 98% yield (see NMR data in Supplementary material).

To a solution of GQD (30mg) in CH2Cl2, EDC·HCl (0.134mmol) and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP, 0.134mmol) were added and the mixture was left under stirring at room temperature for 30min. 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBt, 0.134mmol) was added and the mixture was stirred for 30min. Then, a solution of BTN (0.134mmol) in CH2Cl2 (10mL) was added and the suspension was left under stirring for 4days at room temperature. The resulting material was washed several times with CH2Cl2 and water and centrifuged at 3000rpm until no organic materials were present in the washing solutions and dried at 60°C under vacuum.

A solution of GQD-BTN or GQD (30mg) in deionized water was stirred with a solution of doxorubicin hydrochloride (10mg) in 10mL of basic buffer solution at pH 7.4, at room temperature for 48h. The solution was then centrifuged at 3000rpm and the resulting material was washed several times with water until no drug was present in the washing solutions and dried at 60°C under vacuum. The amount of unbound DOX was determined by measuring the absorbance at 490nm, relative to a calibration curve recorded under the same conditions. The drug loading for GQD-BTN-DOX and GQD-DOX were found to be 16.6wt% and 17,8% respectively.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patters were carried out on a Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer, using the CuKα1 radiation. The GQD morphology was analyzed using an HRTEM JEOL JEM 2010 analytical electron microscope (LaB6 electron gun) operating at 200kV and equipped with a Gatan 794 Multi-Scan CCD camera for digital imaging placing a drop of the GQD dispersed in isopropanol on 400mesh holey carbon coated copper grids. The particle sizing measurements were performed by DLS technique on diluted water solutions of GQD at room temperature (20°C) using a homemade apparatus equipped with APD detectors and a multi-τ digital time correlator (LS Instrument AG). The excitation source was a He–Ne laser emitting at a wavelength of 632.8nm in linearly polarized single-frequency mode. The scattering angle was fixed at 90°. The correlation data have been treated with CONTIN analysis (LS Instrument) reporting intensity averaged size values. Three water solutions at different dilution were investigated and 5 correlation functions were processed for each sample. Micro Raman scattering measurements were performed in back scattering geometry with a Jobin-Yvon HR Evolution Raman spectrometer equipped with a Microscope Olympus (BX-41) and a CCD as detector at room temperature and using the laser excitation of 785nm. The photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out on a NanoLog modular spectrofluorometer Horiba with a Xe lamp as the excitation light source at room temperature; GQD based nanomaterials and free DOX used for PL measurement were used at the concentration of 100ng/mL. 1NMR spectra were recorded at 500MHz Varian instrument; chemical shifts are given in ppm from TMS as internal standard. Preparative separations were made by flash column chromatography using Merck silica gel 0.063–0.200mm and 0.035–0.070mm. Thermogravimetrical studies were performed from 100 to 1000°C at 10°C/min under nitrogen atmosphere on a TA Q500 instrument. UV spectra have been performed by Thermo Nicolet mod, Evolution 500 spectrophotometer; the UV calibration curve of DOX was performed using drug concentrations ranging from 10 to 100ng/mL. FTIR measurements were carried out using a Bruker Vertex 80V FTIR spectrometer equipped with a Bruker Platinum ATR accessory with a single reflection diamond crystal.

Confluent monolayers of A549 cells were used to assess biocompatibility of the carriers and the drug uptake of GQD-DOX and GQD-BTN-DOX, by employing FACS technique. Briefly, cells were treated with the suspensions of GQD prepared in cell medium (with 2% FCS) for the time required in the experiments. After incubation, the cells were detached with trypsin (0.25% trypsin 1mM EDTA), pooled with the corresponding supernatants, washed and suspended in PBS (∼5×104 mL−1).

To detect biocompatibility of the carriers, suspensions of treated cells (∼5×104 mL−1) were loaded with propidium iodine (3μg/mL) and the dead stained cells were recorded in the FL-3 channel (maximum emission signals 617nm). To evaluate the drug uptake in cells treated with doxorubicin loaded GQD, emission signal of the drug (560–590nm) in cell suspensions was recorded in the FL-2 channel. Cells, maintained in the same medium with the addition of PBS or with doxorubicin solutions, were included as negative and positive controls respectively. Doxorubicin solutions were used to the same doses loaded in GQD (range 3.75–15μg/mL). To measure the mortality rate due to intercalating drug, the cell counts were detected cytofluorimetrically since the interference of GQD prevented to perform the more used colorimetric viability tests (e.g. MTT) (Romeo et al., 2013).

The mortality rate due to intercalating drug was assessed by comparing the cell counts in treated cells with control cells. Briefly, after treatment of the confluent monolayers for the experimental times, suspensions formed by detached cells were counted after selection of cell populations by forward and side scatter parameters. All the biological experiments were performed in triplicate and means±SD were calculated. Pearson correlation coefficient was calculated to assess the relationship between uptake of drug loaded GQD and cell mortality.

The GQD, synthesized by prolonged acidic oxidation of pristine MWCNT (p-MWCNT), were characterized by XRD,TEM, CONTIN analysis of DLS, PL and Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). As confirmed by XRD analysis, the synthesized nanomaterial shows no diffraction signals in the whole 2 theta range and the disappearing of the (002) XRD peak, which is present in the precursor p-MWCNT and usually represents a measure of interplanar spacing for two neighbouring graphene sheets (Lin and Zhang, 2012). This result confirmed the thin structure of the synthesized nanomaterial which, after acidic exfoliation, clearly lost the interplannar spacing (Fig. 2A).

The morphology and size of nanoparticles to be used as drug carriers can greatly influence the cell uptake. Nanomaterials in small particle size have the potential for crossing various biological barriers and allow for access into cells and various cellular compartments, including the nucleus (Chen et al., 2010). The use of MWCNT as graphene source allowed us to obtain graphene nanodots with low particle size. The representative TEM images of GQD showed homogeneously distributed nanoparticles with circular shape and diameter less than 5nm (Fig. 2B). The HRTEM image indicated a crystalline graphene structure (inset in Fig. 2B). The presence of a graphene structure, evidenced by TEM analysis and not detectable by XRD due to the very small particle size of GQD, allows the multimodal conjugation of these carbon based nanoparticles.

From the CONTIN analysis of DLS data, two different populations can be identified: one is centered at 9.5nm (±0.5nm), while the second population has an average hydrodynamic radius of 50.5nm. The peak area ratio indicates that the larger size population is still very poorly crowded at this stage (12%) with respect to the smaller size population (88%). However, the width of both the distributions that are approximately gaussians, is indicative of some polydispersity in particle size. Negligible changes in the particle size distribution were observed by varying the dilution, indicating that all measurements were indeed performed in a dilute regime (Fig. 2C).

The uniformity of the GQD particle size distribution was also confirmed by PL spectra since, even exciting the GQD dispersions at wavelengths from 310 to 370nm, always a strong peak at 560nm, can be observed (Fig. 2D). Their stable photoluminescence properties represent a main strength for these materials, allowing their efficient tracking in human cells in vitro.

The Raman spectrum of GQD shows the presence of the D (1320cm−1) and G peaks (1594cm−1) of aromatic domainswith D peak more intense than the G one, when compared to the parent MWCNT, thus confirming the loss of long range order after the strong oxidative treatment (Supplementary Fig. 1, ESI). Taken all together, these results encouraged us to employ the synthesized GQD as nanocarriers for the targeted delivery of anticancer drugs.

The possibility offered by The GQD multimodal conjugation was exploited for the insertion of the tumor targeting ligand unit biotin. The amount of carboxylic groups involved in the conjugation reaction was evaluated by Boehm titration and was found to be 2.0mmol/g. GQD were linked to the vitamin by means of a strategically designed biocompatible and cleavable linker which can be specifically activated inside cells, thus improving improve the therapeutic response. Biotin was conjugated with the bidentate linker 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy) bis(ethylamine), protected with the tert-butyloxycarbonyl protecting (BOC) group, at a single amine functionality. The coupling reaction of the carboxylic group present on the biotin molecule and the free nucleophilic amino group of the linker was performed using EDC/HOBt as coupling agents; the subsequent deprotection of the amino group afforded the biotin module BTN which was purified by chromatography and characterized by NMR spectroscopy (Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3, ESI).

GQD were then conjugated with BTN by coupling reaction of the free carboxylic groups present on the GQD surface amino group of BTN using also in this case, EDC/HOBt as coupling agents. The 1H NMR spectrum of the GQD-Biotin conjugate shows the presence of aromatic protons in the range 7.4–7.9ppm, attributable to sp2 hybridized graphene QD surface and the additional resonance at 8.55ppm ascribable to the newly formed NH amidic bond (Fig. 4
, ESI). Moreover, as expected, a general shift of all signals to +δ values, due to the deshielding effect of aromatic rings, can be observed (Zyta et al., 2016).

The recent evidences that graphene quantum dots can efficiently accelerate the nuclear accumulation of DNA interfering drugs such as doxorubicin or cisplatin enhancing the DNA cleavage activity and drug cytotoxicity, prompted us to use doxorubicin as model intercalating drug. The drug was loaded by π-π interaction to the GQD surface, taking advantage of the excellent absorption properties of these graphene based materials (Fig. 3). The choice of this drug was also motivated by its inherent fluorescence which allows the drug uptake to be tracked. The loading with doxorubicin hydrochloride was performed in basic buffer solution at pH 7.4 in order to allow the effective π-π stacking interaction with the graphene QD surface, as it is reported in literature for similar systems (Liu et al., 2008).

The amount of doxorubicin loaded on the GQD-BTN-DOX was assessed by UV spectroscopy as difference between the initial amount of drug mixed with the nanomaterial and the amount of unbound drug present in the precipitate after the centrifugation and washing steps and using the UV absorbance at 490nm, relative to a calibration curve generated using the same conditions. The system showed a drug loading value of 16.6wt%. This value is comparable with the drug loading values reported in literature for GQD based materials (Qiu et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2013).

The GQD-BTN-DOX system was characterized by TGA, FTIR and PL analyses in order to verify the covalent linkage of biotin and the stable loading of doxorubicin on the GQD surface (Figs. 4 and 5 ). TGA spectra of GQD-BTN-DOX and its precursor performed under inert atmosphere showed an increase of the weight loss (30% for GQD-BTN and 45% for GQD-BTN-DOX) after each synthetic step which is directly correlated to the increase of organic mass loaded on the GQD surface; moreover, a different shapes of TGA profiles related to GQD-BTN and GQD-BTN-DOX samples can be observed (Fig. 4A), thus confirming that a deep chemical modification occurred on the nanomaterial. The results of this analysis further confirmed the results obtained by Boehm titration and gave us information about the BTN loading on GQD. In fact, as reported in literature (Vázquez-Nakagawa et al., 2016), at 600°C under inert atmosphere, the removal of the stable carboxylic acid groups at the edges of the GQD structure is ensured. The weight loss observed for the synthesized GQD at 600°C under inert atmosphere is of 8wt%, that is 1.8mmol/g, which roughly corresponds to the value obtained by Boehm titration. The thermogravimetrical analysis allowed us also to estimate the BTN loading on GQD, which was calculated at 1000°C, under inert atmosphere, as difference between the percentages of weight losses observed between the GQD-BTN sample and its precursor. The observed difference that is of 17%, corresponds to 0.45mmol/g. The FTIR spectra of GQD-BTN and GQD-BTN-DOX samples,compared to the precursors GQD are showed in Fig. 4B. In the GQD sample, the strong broad peaks at around 3450cm−1 and 1620cm−1 associated with the vibrations of OH and CO bonds respectively can be observed; the peak at 1072cm−1 can be related to CO alkoxy groups present on the nanomaterial. These data indicated that many oxygenated functional groups are present on the GQD surfaces, making them very soluble in water. The FTIR spectrum of the conjugate sample GQD-BTN shows the additional representative peak at 1660cm−1 due to the CO stretch of the amide functionality present on the sample after the biotin module conjugation, a wide absorbance at 3050–3200cm−1 due to the NH stretching and a broad peak at 1450cm−1, due to the NH bending. For GQD-BTN-DOX sample, an increase in the peak at 1450cm−1 and a more intense broad peak at 3450–3550cm−1 due to the doxorubicin OH and NH bonds, can be observed.

The presence of a stable interaction of the drug with the GQD surface was confirmed by PL spectra. As reported, the fluorescence of small molecules can be quenched because of their π-π stacking with graphene surfaces. The photoluminescence properties of GQD after MWCNT acidic exfoliation as well as the PL spectra of the GQD-BTN and GQD-BTN-DOX derivatives and DOX, at the excitation wavelength of 360nm in deionized water, are shown in Fig. 5. The fluorescence intensity of DOX decreased in the DOX loaded system GQD-BTN-DOX system from 600 to 570nm, thus indicating that a chemical interaction between the drug and the GQD based nanomaterial occurred. The fluorescence intensity of DOX decreases with the increase of the GQD concentration and as reported in literature, it can be quenched at the DOX/GQD ratio of 0.04 (Wang et al., 2013). The quenching of fluorescence intensity observed for the GQD-BTN-DOX sample is in agreement with the investigated DOX/GQD ratio of 0.16.

The ability of the functionalized GQD to deliver doxorubicin was tested on A549, a human lung epithelial cancer cell line (Fig. 6 ). The first step of biological experiments was aimed to check the biocompatibility of the GQD that was assessed by cell viability rate after treatment for 24h, at dose interval of 10–100μg/mL (Fig. 5, ESI).

Viability, expressed as the percentage of viable cells in comparison to the untreated control cells, remained above 85% up to 75μg/mL in both GQD and GQD-BTN treated cells (Fig. 6A). Almost overlapped results were obtained extending the treatment up to 48h (data not shown), highlighting that the synthesized vehicles were harmless.

In order to investigate the ability of the nanosystem to cross cancer cell membrane and release the DNA intercalating drug into the nucleus, we have performed cytofluorimetric analyses of A549 cell suspensions treated with GQD-BTN-DOX, the GQD loaded with the drug and lacking of the biotin recognition molecule and with the free drug at the same doses loaded in GQD (i.e. 3.75–15μg/mL). Time course underlined a fast and marked uptake starting from 3h of incubation.

The measure of emission signals in cell suspensions on the FL-2 channel showed that the uptake enhanced overtime and reached its maximum after 6h; then, it lowered because of increased cells mortality. As shown in Fig. 6B, the emission value in GQD-BTN-DOX treated cells was 27% higher in comparison to free DOX and GQD-DOX treated cells, thus underlining the key role of biotin functionalization to enhance the cancer cell uptake of the drug loaded GQD. However, cell mortality, which was strongly related to uptake (P <0.001), was more delayed after treatment with drug loaded nanoparticles in comparison to the cells treated with the free drug.

Noteworthy, as detected by CLSM images after treatment for 4h, the doxorubicin loaded nanoparticles were preferentially located in the cytoplasm while cells treated with free DNA intercalating drug showed strongly fluorescent nuclei (Fig. 7 ). Moreover, the fluorescence of GQD-BTN-DOX was not uniformly spread across the cytoplasm; it was thickened in endosomal compartment where, following the internalization endocytosis-mediated, the nanoparticles were concentrated. However this phase was transitory and after 6h, a marked nuclear fluorescence in GQD-BTN-DOX treated cells was observed. This delayed release can be addressed to the needful drug detachment from nanoparticles, triggered by the low pH of the endosomal compartment where, after internalization endocytosis-mediated, the nanoparticles were concentrated.

The selective pH-triggered drug release mechanism was also confirmed by abiotic experiments on the GQD-DOX system. Results of these studies showed no drug release at basic (pH 9) or neutral conditions (pH 7.4). At pH 5.3, a burst drug release within 5min was observed, thus confirming a selective pH-triggered drug release mechanism.

In summary, from this study emerged the improved delivery of conventional chemotherapeutics whose activity is restricted by resistance mechanisms and dose limiting side-effects, by using GQD as nanocarrier. These nanomaterials have shown a great biocompatibility and ability to release drugs into cancer cells. Moreover, the possibility offered by their multimodal conjugation allows to incorporate both drugs and targeting ligands in the same nanostructure in order to minimize the anticancer drugs systemic toxicity and undesiderable side effects, tipically associated with conventional chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2016.12.060.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalized single-walled carbon nanotubes as rationally designed vehicles for tumor-targeted drug delivery
Chemical reduction of graphene oxide to graphene by sulfur-containing compounds
Quantum-dot-conjugated graphene as a probe for simultaneous cancer-targeted fluorescent imaging, tracking, and monitoring drug delivery
Influence of carbon source and Fe-catalyst support on the growth of multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Functionalization of multi-walled carbon nanotubes with coumarin derivatives and their biological evaluation
Recent advances in carbon nanotubes as delivery systems for anticancer drugs
Synthesis and anti-HIV activity of carboxylated and drug-conjugated multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Chemical Functionalization of Carbon Nanomaterials: Chemistry and Applications
Scrolls and nested tubes in multiwall carbon nanotubes
Creating high yield water soluble luminescent graphene quantum dots via exfoliating and disintegrating carbon nanotubes and graphite flakes
PEGylated nanographene oxide for delivery of water-Insoluble cancer drugs
The smart drug delivery system and its clinical potential
Nanomaterials in combating cancer: therapeutic applications and developments
Standardization of the Boehm titration: part II method of agitation: effect of filtering and dilute titrant
